Claude Fable 5 vs Kimi K3

7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Solen Feyissa on UnsplashThis is the comparison the benchmark report quietly frames as the race at the top of the frontier: Moonshot AI’s Kimi K3, the first open-weight model in the 3-trillion-parameter class, against Claude Fable 5, Anthropic’s most capable public model, and the only one on the chart K3 couldn’t catch.
The two models couldn’t be more different in origin. K3 launched on July 16, 2026 as a fully documented open release . Fable 5 arrived a month earlier as the public version of Anthropic’s “Mythos-class” model, a system previously restricted to US government cybersecurity partners under the Glasswing program, redeployed for general availability after export controls were lifted .
One is an open book. The other is a black box with a five-week head start. Here’s how they actually stack up.

The Contenders at a Glance

• Kimi K3: 2.8T total parameters, sparse MoE, native vision, 1M-token context, open weights (hosted-only until the checkpoint drops July 27), API at $3/$15 per million tokens .

• Claude Fable 5: Anthropic’s flagship, positioned for “demanding reasoning and long-horizon agentic work,” available through the Claude API at $10/$50 per million tokens, exactly double Opus 4.8 and the priciest model in this entire comparison .
A quirk worth knowing: Fable 5 and the restricted Mythos 5 are the same underlying model. Fable 5 simply layers safety classifiers on cybersecurity and biology queries, matched requests get rerouted to Opus 4.8, which Anthropic says triggers in under 5% of sessions . So when you benchmark “Fable 5,” you’re really benchmarking Anthropic’s government-grade model with a chaperone.

Configurations: Full Disclosure vs Black Box
The configurational contrast between these two is mostly a story about what’s knowable.
What we know about K3, essentially everything:

• 2.8T total parameters, 896 experts, 16 active per token (~1.8% sparsity) via Stable LatentMoE .

• Kimi Delta Attention hybrid linear attention, claimed 6.3× faster decoding at 1M context .

• Gated MLA for KV compression, Attention Residuals for training efficiency, ~2.5× the scaling efficiency of K2 .

• MXFP4 weights / MXFP8 activations, quantization-aware from the SFT stage .
What we know about Fable 5 essentially nothing:

• No published parameter count, no architecture details, no verified context-window figure in Anthropic’s checked materials .

• No downloadable weights, no license to inspect it’s a hosted API and nothing more .
This isn’t a criticism so much as a fork in philosophy. Moonshot is betting that transparency accelerates adoption; Anthropic is betting that its safety-and-reliability stack is the product. If your procurement process requires knowing what you’re running, only one of these models can satisfy it.

Memory Footprint and Deployment
With Fable 5, the deployment section is one sentence long: there isn’t one. You call Anthropic’s API (or access it through Claude.ai subscriptions), and Anthropic’s infrastructure handles everything .
K3, by contrast, has a real if demanding, self-hosting story:
~1.4TB of MXFP4 weights before KV cache, MXFP8 activations .
Moonshot recommends supernode deployments of 64+ accelerators, and contributed a custom vLLM path because KDA breaks standard prefix caching .
So “footprint” here means two different things. Fable 5’s footprint is zero hardware and total vendor dependence. K3’s footprint is a small datacenter, but it’s your datacenter, with your data never leaving the building. For regulated industries and sovereign deployments, that distinction alone may decide the comparison before a single benchmark is run.

Pricing: A 3× Gap at the Sticker
The sticker prices are not close:
Kimi K3: $3.00/M input, $0.30/M cached input, $15.00/M output .
Claude Fable 5: $10/M input, ~$1/M cached, $50/M output .
K3 is roughly 70% cheaper on both sides of the meter . On caching-heavy agentic workloads, where Moonshot reports 90%+ cache-hit rates on programming traffic, the effective gap widens further, since K3’s cached input is a tenth of its already-lower base rate .
Cost per completed task softens the blow somewhat for Anthropic. Artificial Analysis measured K3 at $0.94 per task on its long-horizon suite, competitive with GPT-5.6 Sol’s $1.04 and half of Opus 4.8’s $1.80 . Fable 5’s per-task economics will be harsher than all of them at $50/M output, and third-party analysts explicitly warn that agentic workflows multiply Fable 5 calls through planning, tool use, retries, and verification loops . Anthropic’s own prompt caching can cut input costs ~90%, which helps, but output tokens are where agent bills live .
The honest framing: Fable 5 is priced as a premium reasoning layer, not a default engine, even analysts friendly to Anthropic recommend routing routine steps to cheaper models and reserving Fable 5 for decisions that are expensive to get wrong . K3 is priced like it wants to be your default.

The Benchmarks : What They Mean and Who Wins
All results come from the benchmark report, every model maxed out on thinking effort. Quick decoder:

• GDPval-AA v2 (Elo): economically valuable knowledge work, judged head-to-head, chess-style ratings.

• AA-Briefcase (Elo): document-heavy analyst work.

• JobBench: multi-step job tasks executed end to end.

• SpreadsheetBench 2: real spreadsheet manipulation.

• Automation Bench: long-horizon autonomous task completion.

• BrowseComp: hard web retrieval and research.

• CharXiv / Zerobench (Visual Agents): chart understanding and visual reasoning with tools; Zerobench is pass@5.

General agents
Fable 5 takes the crown, but K3 is the only model that makes it uncomfortable

• GDPval-AA v2: Fable 5 1760 vs K3 1668. A 92-Elo gap real, but K3 sits 68 points clear of third-place GPT-5.6 Sol. These two are in a tier of their own.

• AA-Briefcase: Fable 5 1583 vs K3 1548, just 35 points, the closest Elo result on the chart. K3’s document work is effectively at Fable’s level.

• JobBench: Fable 5 57.4 vs K3 52.9. Fable wins; K3 again beats everything else.

• SpreadsheetBench 2: K3 34.8 vs Fable 5 34.7. A statistical dead heat, with K3 taking the top spot by a tenth of a point.

• Automation Bench: K3 30.8 vs Fable 5 29.1, K3’s clearest general-agent win, and the only chart where Fable 5 doesn’t even medal (GPT-5.6 Sol slips between them at 29.7).

• BrowseComp: K3 91.2 vs Fable 5 88.0. K3 posts the best research-and-retrieval score of any model tested.

Visual agents
CharXiv (RQ) w/ tool: Fable 5 93.5 vs K3 91.3 ; Fable’s strongest visual result, and the top score on that chart.
Zerobench (Pass@5): Fable 5 46.0 vs K3 41.0 ; a five-point gap, though K3 still ties GPT-5.5 for second.
Final tally across eight benchmarks: Fable 5 wins five, K3 wins three.
Fable 5 is the better model overall, and K3 is the only open-weight system close enough to split podiums with it, beating GPT-5.6 Sol and Opus-4.8 nearly everywhere in the process.

The Pattern Behind the Numbers
The split is remarkably clean.
Fable 5 wins where judgment dominates: knowledge-work Elo, analyst tasks, chart reading, visual reasoning. These are benchmarks where taste, calibration, and reliability matter more than raw persistence historically Anthropic’s home turf.
K3 wins where autonomy and retrieval dominate: Automation Bench (long-horizon unsupervised work), BrowseComp (digging obscure facts out of the web), and SpreadsheetBench 2 (mechanical precision at scale). That profile matches its architecture, a 2.8T-parameter knowledge pool, linear-attention decoding built to stay coherent over very long trajectories, and a 1M-token window documented in its official platform materials .
Early hands-on testing echoes the split. In one side-by-side coding bake-off, “Fable 5 finished faster, and most UX components were more robust and easy to use,” while “Kimi K3 was much more complex and visually appealing” . Fable is the safer pair of hands; K3 is the more ambitious swing.

Caveats Worth Knowing
Fable 5’s numbers come with routing fine print. Cybersecurity and biology queries get silently handed to Opus 4.8, under 5% of sessions, Anthropic says, but if your workload is security research, you may not be benchmarking the model you think you are .
K3’s openness is still loading. Weights are committed for July 27; until then it’s hosted-only, and every serving statistic — 62 output tokens/sec, ~2s time-to-first-token, comes from Moonshot’s own infrastructure .
Verification is asymmetric. Fable 5 has five weeks of third-party scrutiny behind it; K3’s benchmark record is days old, and independent testing is still thin . Both models’ headline figures deserve a controlled bake-off on your own tasks before any migration.
Neither is cheap to run at max effort. This report maxes thinking effort on both. K3 currently only runs at max reasoning with fixed sampling ; Fable 5 at $50/M output will punish verbose agent loops . Budget for the thinking, not just the answer.

Which One Should You Choose?
Pick Kimi K3 if you:

• Need frontier-class agentic performance at roughly 30% of Fable 5’s token price .

• Run autonomous, long-horizon, or retrieval-heavy workloads the benchmarks say K3 is the best in the world at these right now.

• Require open weights, self-hosting, or on-prem data control (once the checkpoint lands) .

• Want a documented 1M-token context and native multimodality in one architecture .
Pick Claude Fable 5 if you:

• Want the single strongest model on the chart for knowledge work and visual reasoning and can pay for it.

• Value Anthropic’s mature enterprise ecosystem, documentation, and safety stack .

• Need a model you can deploy today with zero infrastructure thinking.

• Are doing high-stakes work where Anthropic’s classifier layer is a feature, not a limitation.

Verdict
Claude Fable 5 is the better model. Kimi K3 is the better deal and the more interesting one.
Fable 5 wins the benchmark report five-to-three and holds the top Elo on both knowledge-work suites, justifying its premium for teams where being right is worth more than being cheap. But K3 beats it on autonomy, retrieval, and spreadsheets, ties it on document work, and does all of it at 70% lower token prices with open weights on the way . A year ago, the question “can an open model sit at the same table as Anthropic’s flagship?” was hypothetical.
The benchmark report answers it: K3 isn’t just at the table it’s the only other model in the room.
